One of the most significant barriers to treatment is the body dysmorphia associated with the condition, where the sufferer often cannot see themselves as dangerously thin. On the contrary, they perceive their weight as either ideal or completely normal and may not believe they need help. Therefore, it can be difficult for the individual to understand when loved ones try to encourage them to seek help, leading them to distrust those around them.
AnorexiaNervosa.signsTitle: What are the signs of anorexia nervosa?
AnorexiaNervosa.signsDescription: Identifying the signs in time is the first step toward providing the necessary support and saving lives. These indicators often manifest through drastic changes in eating behavior, self-perception, and physical well-being. Understanding these signs allows us to act with empathy and professionalism in the face of a complex reality.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item2: Persistent fear of gaining weight or becoming fat, despite being underweight.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item3: Seeing oneself as overweight or being overly focused on body shape and size, even when underweight.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item4: Severe restriction of food intake, often involving extremely low-calorie diets, fasting, or avoidance of certain foods.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item5: Menstrual irregularities or complete loss of periods.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item6: Dizziness, fainting and feeling cold all the time.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item7: Excessive hair growth on the chest, face and arms.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptoms2Title: What are the psychological symptoms of anorexia nervosa?
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item1: Constant thoughts about food, calories, dieting, and body weight.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item2: Feelings of worthlessness or inadequacy, often tied to body weight and appearance.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item3: An extreme drive for perfection, especially regarding body image and eating habits.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item4: Persistent feelings of sadness, hopelessness, or loss of interest in previously enjoyed activities.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item5: High levels of anxiety, particularly related to eating, body image, and social situations involving food.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item6: Distorted perception of one’s body and an intense fear of weight gain.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item7: Feelings of failure, disgust, shame, guilt and low self-esteem.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item8: Rigid thinking and a strong need to control.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item9: A lack of emotional capacity or expression, feeling ‘numb’ a lot of the time.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptoms3Title: What are the physical symptoms of anorexia nervosa?
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item1: Chronic tiredness and lack of energy due to malnutrition.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item2: Hair loss, thinning hair, or brittle nails due to nutrient deficiencies.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item3: Development of fine, downy hair on the body, a response to low body fat.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item4: Sensitivity to cold, often feeling cold even in warm environments due to low body fat.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item5: Dry skin, bruising easily, and poor wound healing.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item6: Frequent illnesses due to a weakened immune system.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item7: Swelling of the feet or cold, mottled hands.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptoms4Title: What are the behavioural symptoms of anorexia nervosa?
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item2: Unusual eating habits such as cutting food into tiny pieces, eating very slowly, or rearranging food on the plate.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item3: Excessive or obsessive exercise to burn calories, even when exhausted or injured.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item4: Avoidance of social situations, especially those involving food, to maintain restrictive eating patterns.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item5: Refusal to acknowledge feelings of hunger, often claiming not to be hungry despite obvious undernutrition.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item6: Stashing food away or hiding it to avoid eating in front of others or to maintain control over food intake.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item7: Obsessive preoccupation with weight, calories, food, dieting and fat grams.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item8: Increasing refusal to eat certain foods or categories of food.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item9: Frequent feelings or comments about feeling fat despite weight loss.
AnorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item10: Excessive sleep and a lack of interest in once-loved activities or events.
AnorexiaNervosa.howWeTreatDescription:
At Gabriel Arcángel, we understand that anorexia is a complex and isolating condition. We know that each person’s journey with anorexia is unique; therefore, we do not follow a ‘one-size-fits-all’ approach. Your stay with us will include a bespoke treatment plan, carefully structured to meet your specific needs. We cannot tell you exactly what your four-week stay will look like because your path to recovery will be as individual as you are.
Each day will be meticulously designed to include a variety of therapeutic elements that work together, addressing the physical, emotional, and psychological aspects of anorexia. Our multidisciplinary team will be with you every step of the way, supporting you in understanding the underlying causes of your condition, restoring your physical health, and helping you develop a more compassionate relationship with food and your body.
AnorexiaNervosa.section1Description:
We treat anorexia nervosa with a 360-degree approach. Alongside extensive psychoeducation, our treatment is a unique combination of intensive psychotherapy, biochemical restoration, and numerous complementary therapies.
Our therapeutic approach focuses on understanding your relationship with food and the psychological issues driving it. We perform meticulous testing to identify any co-occurring conditions, such as anxiety, depression, or trauma, that may be influencing your eating disorder. These conditions are often interwoven with anorexia, and we believe that treating them simultaneously is crucial for lasting recovery.
Through this comprehensive understanding of your unique situation, we design a personalized treatment plan that not only stabilizes weight and nutritional health but also addresses the fundamental psychological factors that led to the development of anorexia.
A key part of our approach is the development of long-term behavioral changes. Our goal is not to take control away from you, but to help you regain it in a healthy and sustainable way. We empower you to rebuild trust in your body, challenge harmful thought patterns, and learn new coping strategies that support a balanced and fulfilling life. Every step of the way, we are here to support you with care, empathy, and expertise, helping you rediscover who you were before, and who you are beyond the anorexia.
AnorexiaNervosa.section2Description: Our team is composed of doctors, therapists, and specialists. This group of experts will be exclusively dedicated to you and will be by your side throughout your entire stay at our anorexia nervosa treatment center. This includes a personal psychiatrist, a primary care physician, a nutritionist, a patient care team, and many other professionals. At the heart of this team is a live-in therapist, available to provide emotional support and help you manage intrusive thoughts 24/7 throughout your stay.
AnorexiaNervosa.section3Description:
We design our comprehensive anorexia nervosa treatment programs to fit your individual needs. At every stage, you can expect cutting-edge therapies delivered with medical excellence.
We can provide treatment for anorexia nervosa and any co-occurring mental health conditions at our treatment center in Querétaro.

BulimiaNervosa.conditionHeading: Bulimia Nervosa: Understanding, Indicators, and Therapeutic Approach
BulimiaNervosa.conditionDescription:
We understand that bulimia nervosa can feel relentless. The binge-purge cycle can be all-consuming and lead to numerous adverse consequences. While seeking help may seem daunting, recovery is possible, and we can help you achieve it.
Our team will guide you through every step of the recovery process, helping you break free from that cycle and rebuild your relationship with food and with yourself.
BulimiaNervosa.whatIsDescription:
Bulimia is a highly complex and potentially life-threatening eating disorder. Those who suffer from bulimia secretly consume large amounts of food and lose control over their eating habits—a process known as ‘bingeing’ — only to later ‘purge’ their bodies of this food through vomiting, the use of laxatives, excessive exercise, or fasting. Purging is almost always done in secret. Like all addictions and mental health conditions, symptoms can escalate rapidly: the intervals between bingeing and purging episodes shorten, and the time dedicated to these behaviors increases.
The causes of an eating disorder depend on the specific circumstances of each person’s life. Individuals suffering from bulimia often exhibit personality traits such as neuroticism, obsessiveness, and perfectionism. Undiagnosed co-occurring mental illnesses, unresolved trauma, low self-esteem, a harsh inner critic, genetics, and learned behaviors (from a parent, sibling, or caregiver during childhood) can also contribute to the development of bulimia.
Bulimia has a devastating impact on both physical and mental health. Risks include: kidney failure, dehydration, heart problems, severe tooth decay or gum disease, absent or irregular menstrual cycles, digestive issues, depression and anxiety, substance abuse, self-harm, and suicidal ideation. It is not uncommon for a person’s initial struggle with anorexia or binge-eating disorder to evolve into bulimia.
BulimiaNervosa.signsTitle: What are the signs of bulimia nervosa?
BulimiaNervosa.signsDescription: Since a person suffering from bulimia rarely exhibits the same signs of malnutrition as someone with anorexia, it can be very difficult to detect. The tell-tale signs are usually puffy cheeks, tooth discoloration, weight fluctuations, bloodshot eyes, and calluses on the knuckles from induced vomiting.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom1Item1: Consuming an excessive amount of food in a short period while feeling a loss of control.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom1Item2: Purging through forced vomiting after binge eating.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom1Item3: Constantly thinking about and planning the next binge and purge cycle, with relief only coming after purging.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom1Item4: Intense feelings of remorse or embarrassment related to eating habits.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom1Item5: Experiencing mood disturbances, including sadness, anger, and social withdrawal.
BulimiaNervosa.symptoms2Title: What are the psychological symptoms of bulimia?
BulimiaNervosa.symptom2Item1: Only feeling ‘calm’ after a period of purging.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom2Item2: Having a distorted and increasingly negative body image.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom2Item3: Feeling a complete loss of control during periods of binge eating.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom2Item4: Restricting calories, fasting, or avoiding certain foods altogether between binges.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom2Item5: Use of substances as a coping mechanism, leading to irritability.
BulimiaNervosa.symptoms3Title: What are the physical symptoms of bulimia?
BulimiaNervosa.symptom3Item1: Regularly inducing vomiting to purge food from the body.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom3Item2: Using diuretics, dietary supplements, enemas, or laxatives after eating in an attempt to lose weight
BulimiaNervosa.symptom3Item3: Excessive exercise in an attempt to lose weight
BulimiaNervosa.symptom3Item4: More frequent and prolonged episodes of bingeing and purging.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom3Item5: Repeatedly eating excessive quantities of food in one sitting, especially foods the person denies themselves in between binges.
BulimiaNervosa.symptoms4Title: What are the emotional symptoms of bulimia?
BulimiaNervosa.symptom4Item1: Intense emotions related to eating, leading to a cycle of bingeing and purging.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom4Item2: Emotional withdrawal and intense mood swings.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom4Item3: Obsessing over binges and purging – emotional relief that comes only after purging.
BulimiaNervosa.symptom4Item4: Feeling a complete loss of control during periods of binge eating (emotional overwhelm and helplessness during binge episodes.)
BulimiaNervosa.symptom4Item5: Emotional instability linked with substance use as a coping mechanism.
BulimiaNervosa.howWeTreatDescription:
At Gabriel Arcángel, we understand that bulimia nervosa is a complex disorder that affects both your physical and emotional well-being. We cannot tell you exactly what your four-week stay will involve because every patient receives a bespoke treatment plan, tailored to their unique needs. Your treatment program will be thoughtfully structured, combining a variety of therapeutic elements designed to support you in breaking the binge-purge cycle and addressing the underlying causes of your condition.
Our therapies are specifically chosen to complement one another, ensuring that every aspect of your health is addressed: physically, emotionally, and psychologically. Whether through psychotherapy to address emotional triggers, medical treatments to restore your physical health, or nutritional support to rebuild a healthy relationship with food, we ensure that every facet of your recovery is supported. See below for more details.
BulimiaNervosa.section1Description:
Our priority is to gain a deep understanding of your triggers and to address any underlying factors contributing to the eating disorder, whether emotional, psychological, or physical. Many of our patients also experience co-occurring conditions, such as anxiety, depression, or trauma, which often exacerbate bulimia. We make it a point to address these conditions in tandem, ensuring a comprehensive and integrated treatment approach. By treating all contributing factors, our goal is to help you develop robust, long-term coping strategies that will support your healing journey.
A key feature of our bulimia treatment program is Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), a highly effective therapy for eating disorders. CBT helps you identify and challenge harmful thought patterns that contribute to the binge-purge cycle and replaces them with healthier, more constructive ways of thinking. Our therapists will work with you to reframe negative beliefs and behaviors, empowering you to take control of your thoughts and actions. Alongside CBT, we integrate medical treatments to restore your physical health and address any physiological imbalances caused by bulimia, such as nutritional deficiencies, dehydration, and electrolyte imbalances. Nutritional therapy is another cornerstone of our approach, helping you rebuild a healthier relationship with food and develop sustainable eating habits.
BulimiaNervosa.section2Description: A dedicated team of experts, composed of doctors, therapists, and bulimia specialists, is here to help you rediscover yourself. A crucial member of this team is a live-in therapist, who will be available to provide emotional support 24/7 throughout the entire program.
BulimiaNervosa.section3Description:
We shape our treatment programs to suit your individual needs. Driven by our principle of humanistic pragmatism, our holistic bulimia treatment programs aim to meet you exactly where you are and provide you with the specific combination of treatment you require.
We can provide treatment for bulimia and any co-occurring mental health conditions with absolute privacy and discretion at our bulimia clinic in Querétaro.

OrthorexiaNervosa.conditionHeading: Orthorexia Nervosa: Understanding, Indicators, and Therapeutic Approach
OrthorexiaNervosa.conditionDescription: At Gabriel Arcángel, we provide comprehensive, bespoke, and expert-designed treatment for orthorexia nervosa to help you recreate a healthy relationship with food. To achieve this goal, our team will address any underlying issues and provide you with the tools necessary to create robust coping strategies.
OrthorexiaNervosa.whatIsDescription:
Orthorexia involves an unhealthy obsession with healthy or ‘clean’ eating. Food groups are typically split into rigid ‘good’ and ‘bad’ categories, coupled with an irrational fear of these ‘bad’ foods. While healthy eating is essential for self-care, it becomes harmful when it turns into an obsession that negatively impacts one's life. Rather than controlling their food consumption to feel healthy, a person suffering from orthorexia uses healthy eating as a way to cope with psychological issues such as depression, anxiety, trauma, or difficult emotions like guilt or sadness.
Numerous factors contribute to the development of orthorexia nervosa. These include genetic dispositions, such as a family history of eating disorders; developmental factors, such as childhood trauma; underlying mental health conditions; low self-esteem; and obsessive personality traits. Environmental factors, such as having a job that emphasizes clean eating or thinness (for example, ballet dancers, athletes, personal trainers, and Instagram influencers whose ‘brand’ is fitness and health), can also influence the likelihood of developing orthorexia nervosa.
Orthorexia can cause permanent damage to physical and psychological health, producing effects similar to those of eating disorders like anorexia or bulimia over time. For example, it can lead to malnutrition, a weakened immune system, reduced bone density, heart and lung damage, kidney failure, pancreatitis, and irregular menstruation. It can also impair a person’s cognitive abilities and emotional well-being, which can lead to substance abuse, other mental illnesses, diminished self-esteem, and suicidal thoughts. If left untreated, orthorexia can lead to other eating disorders, such as anorexia or bulimia.
OrthorexiaNervosa.signsTitle: What are the signs of orthorexia nervosa?
OrthorexiaNervosa.signsDescription: Unlike other eating disorders, individuals with orthorexia are less focused on body image and weight loss. Instead, the disorder manifests as the avoidance of foods due to perceived (usually undiagnosed) allergies, the continuous reduction of acceptable food choices, an irrational concern regarding the food’s origins, and a fear of medical ill-health. Orthorexia can be very difficult to diagnose because cutting out entire food groups—such as sugar, fats, meat, or dairy—is often perceived as healthy.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item1: Feeling intense guilt or shame when consuming ‘bad’ foods.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item2: Spending an excessive amount of time thinking about and planning meals.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item4: Hiding or concealing food from others.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item5: Linking self-esteem with adherence to a specific diet.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item6: Being overly interested and/or critical of other people’s eating habits.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom1Item7: Obsessing over food origins, ingredients, and nutrition labels.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptoms2Title: What are the psychological symptoms of orthorexia?
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item1: Obsessive preoccupation with food purity, quality, and nutritional content.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item2: Anxiety or guilt when eating foods deemed “unhealthy” or “impure.”
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item3: Feelings of moral superiority tied to eating “clean” or “perfect” foods.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item4: Fear of illness or disease from eating “unhealthy” foods.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item5: Low self-esteem or identity tied to food choices.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item6: Emotional distress when “healthy” food is unavailable.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom2Item7: Rigid, black-and-white thinking about food (e.g., labelling foods as “good” or “bad”).
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptoms3Title: What are the physical symptoms of orthorexia?
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item1: Unintentional weight loss due to restrictive eating.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item2: Fatigue or low energy from inadequate caloric intake or nutrient imbalances.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item3: Gastrointestinal issues, such as bloating or constipation, caused by a limited diet.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item4: Malnutrition or deficiencies in vitamins and minerals.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item5: Loss of menstrual periods (amenorrhea) in individuals assigned female at birth.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item6: Brittle hair, nails, or dry skin as a result of inadequate nutrition.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom3Item7: Muscle weakness or reduced physical performance.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptoms4Title: What are the behavioural symptoms of orthorexia?
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item1: Spending excessive amounts of time researching or planning meals.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item2: Avoiding social events or dining out due to fear of eating “impure” food.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item3: Extreme adherence to food rules or rituals (e.g., preparing food in a specific way).
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item4: Refusing to eat food prepared by others due to lack of control over ingredients.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item5: Eliminating entire food groups (e.g., carbs, fats) without medical necessity.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item6: Constantly monitoring or judging others’ eating habits.
OrthorexiaNervosa.symptom4Item7: Progressively narrowing the range of “acceptable” foods.
OrthorexiaNervosa.howWeTreatDescription:
Each individual who comes for a stay at Gabriel Arcángel receives a bespoke treatment plan, with every day thoughtfully structured to include a range of carefully tailored therapeutic elements.
Our treatments work together to address all aspects of orthorexia, from restoring a balanced relationship with food to managing the underlying emotional and psychological struggles. With a focus on both physical and mental well-being, our therapies are designed to complement one another, helping you regain control over your health and build sustainable, positive eating habits.
OrthorexiaNervosa.section1Description:
At Gabriel Arcángel, we treat orthorexia with a multidisciplinary, 360-degree approach. This combines specialized psychotherapy, biochemical restoration, and various complementary therapies.
The first step in your treatment is a thorough and intensive assessment, designed to uncover any underlying issues that may be contributing to your orthorexia, such as emotional trauma, past experiences with food, or biochemical imbalances. Based on these findings, we create an individualized treatment program aimed at addressing the underlying factors and helping you re-establish a healthy relationship with food.
Your treatment program may include evidence-based therapies, such as Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) to help you manage obsessive thoughts surrounding food; trauma-informed care to heal emotional wounds; and nutritional counseling to support a balanced, sustainable approach to eating. Additionally, we provide biochemical restoration treatments, such as hormone balancing or addressing nutrient deficiencies, to restore physical health and support mental clarity.
Complementary therapies like mindfulness, meditation, and relaxation techniques will also be incorporated to help you manage stress and anxiety, rebuild trust in your body, and foster a more flexible relationship with food.
OrthorexiaNervosa.section2Description: A team of professionals will be dedicated to you and by your side throughout your entire stay with us. This includes a personal psychiatrist, medical doctor, dietitian, client relations team, and many others. At the heart of this team is a live-in therapist, available to provide emotional support and help you address every difficult emotion that arises throughout your stay.
OrthorexiaNervosa.section3Description:
Based on our core principles of empathy and pragmatism, we design your treatment for orthorexia nervosa to meet you exactly where you are.
We provide treatment for orthorexia nervosa and any co-occurring mental health conditions at our private orthorexia treatment clinic in Querétaro.

ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.pageHeading: Rehab for Obesity and Metabolic Syndrome
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.conditionHeading: Obesity and Metabolic Syndrome: Diagnosis, Indicators, and Comprehensive Approach
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.conditionDescription: Obesity is a symptom of internal imbalances; it has absolutely nothing to do with willpower. By addressing these underlying issues, our world-renowned specialists can help you lose excess weight and reduce the severity of your symptoms.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.whatIsHeading: What are obesity and metabolic syndromes?
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.whatIsDescription:
Obesity is defined as an abnormal or excessive accumulation of fat that endangers your health. These risks include high blood pressure, elevated LDL cholesterol, Type 2 diabetes, coronary heart disease, stroke, gallbladder disease, mental health issues, osteoarthritis, and many more. In over 60% of cases, obesity is caused by (or causes) metabolic syndrome.
Through our combination of medical, psychological, and personal care, we are proud to be the only clinic in the world that addresses co-occurring conditions and every possible root cause of obesity and metabolic syndrome. In doing so, we can help you recover in a matter of weeks, rather than months or years.
While obesity has reached epidemic proportions in many parts of the world, it remains one of the most stigmatized and misunderstood mental health conditions. The pervasive narrative surrounding obesity is that it is caused by eating too much and moving too little. This narrative is simplistic and unhelpful because, in most cases, obesity is the result of internal imbalances. These can be emotional, psychological, cellular, hormonal, environmental, dietary, and/or genetic. For example, hormonal imbalances such as insulin resistance or leptin deficiency can interfere with the body's ability to regulate hunger and satiety, while emotional factors such as chronic stress or past trauma may lead to overeating as a coping mechanism.
Metabolic syndrome is highly related to obesity, inactivity, and insulin resistance. Insulin resistance means that when you consume food, sugar cannot enter the cells easily. This causes your blood sugar levels to rise, even as your body produces an excessive amount of insulin to try and lower them. Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of conditions that occur simultaneously and increase your risk of developing heart disease, stroke, and Type 2 diabetes.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.signsTitle: What are the signs of obesity and metabolic syndromes?
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.signsDescription: The symptom of obesity is having a body mass index (BMI) of 30 or above. BMI is one of the most widespread tools used to measure body composition and predict how it will impact a person’s health.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptoms1Title: A person is diagnosed with metabolic syndrome if they have three or more of the following symptoms:
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom1Item1: High blood sugar, which may have similar symptoms to diabetes, such as fatigue, blurred vision and increased urination.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom1Item4: Low levels of HDL cholesterol (so-called good cholesterol).
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptoms2Title: What are the physical risks of obesity and metabolic syndrome?
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom2Item1: Cardiovascular diseases: Increased risk of heart attack, stroke, and atherosclerosis.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom2Item2: Type 2 diabetes: Obesity and metabolic syndrome significantly heighten the likelihood of insulin resistance and diabetes.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom2Item3: Joint problems: Excess weight places strain on joints, leading to issues such as osteoarthritis.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom2Item4: Sleep apnea: Fat deposits around the neck can obstruct airways, causing breathing difficulties during sleep.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom2Item5: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD): Fat buildup in the liver can progress to inflammation or scarring.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom2Item6: Certain cancers: Higher risk of cancers such as colorectal, breast (post-menopausal), and endometrial cancer.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom2Item7: Reduced immunity: Chronic inflammation associated with obesity can impair immune function.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptoms3Title: What are the emotional risks of obesity and metabolic syndrome?
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom3Item1: Body image dissatisfaction: Persistent feelings of unhappiness with physical appearance can erode mental well-being.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom3Item2: Low self-esteem: Weight stigma and societal pressures can contribute to feelings of inadequacy.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom3Item3: Anxiety and depression: Chronic stress about weight or health can lead to mental health challenges.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom3Item4: Social isolation: Individuals may withdraw from social situations due to embarrassment or discrimination.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.symptom3Item5: Emotional eating cycles: Using food as a coping mechanism for stress or sadness can perpetuate a negative cycle.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.howWeTreatHeading: How we treat Obesity & Metabolic Syndromes
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.howWeTreatDescription:
We understand that obesity and metabolic syndromes are complex conditions influenced by a variety of factors that go far beyond just willpower or lifestyle choices. That is why we do not use a ‘one-size-fits-all’ approach. Every individual who comes to us receives a bespoke treatment plan tailored to their specific needs. Your four-week stay will be carefully structured, incorporating a range of therapeutic elements designed to support your recovery.
Our treatments work together to address the underlying causes of obesity and metabolic syndromes, including emotional, psychological, and physical factors. We focus on restoring your body's balance, addressing metabolic imbalances, and providing the psychological support needed to help you overcome emotional triggers. See below for more detail.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.section1Description:
At Gabriel Arcángel, our 360-degree treatment approach is centered on restoring balance to both your mind and body. We combine advanced biochemical restoration, intensive psychotherapy, and therapies designed to strengthen the mind-body connection. To ensure that we address every aspect of your condition, you will undergo an in-depth testing process, identifying the root causes of your metabolic imbalances and any physical symptoms you may be experiencing, such as inflammation or hormonal disruptions.
We also assess any co-occurring conditions, such as anxiety, depression, or stress, which may be contributing to or exacerbating your metabolic issues. By understanding the full picture, we can design a treatment program that targets not only your metabolic health but also any underlying emotional or psychological factors that may be affecting your overall well-being.
Based on these results, we create a personalized treatment program designed to target all underlying factors contributing to your obesity or metabolic syndrome. Our medical interventions, including treatments to reduce inflammation, balance hormones, and support metabolic health, are carefully tailored to your unique needs. Alongside this, our psychotherapeutic approaches will support emotional and psychological healing, empowering you to regain control of your health and achieve long-term well-being.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.section2Description: Throughout your treatment program, you will have a dedicated team of doctors, therapists, and obesity specialists. Your team—consisting of medical doctors, nurses, clinical coordinators, a psychologist, a personal trainer, a nutritionist, a live-in therapist, a client relations team, and an in-house hospitality team—will provide unparalleled, 24-hour support to set you on the path to recovery.
ObesityAndMetabolicSyndrome.section3Description:
Guided by our core principles of empathy and pragmatism, we shape an entirely bespoke healing experience. We provide treatment for obesity and any co-occurring mental health conditions at our discrete eating disorders clinic in Querétaro.
We typically recommend a minimum four-week residential program for obesity and metabolic syndrome, ensuring you have sufficient time to address each of your internal imbalances.

BingeEatingDisorder.conditionHeading: Binge Eating Disorder: Diagnosis, Warning Signs, and Therapeutic Approach
BingeEatingDisorder.conditionDescription: No matter who you are, where you are from, or how severe your symptoms are, we can help. Using a tailored-to-you approach, our binge eating disorder treatment programs will address the root causes of your behavior, enabling you to regain control over your relationship with food.
BingeEatingDisorder.whatIsDescription:
Binge Eating Disorder (BED) is a complex eating disorder that often remains undiagnosed for years. While symptoms are unique to each individual, it is generally diagnosed when a person continuously consumes an abnormally high volume of food despite negative consequences to their health, life, relationships, and well-being. Feeling unable to stop this pattern, regardless of how desperately one may wish to, is a typical marker of BED.
Binge Eating Disorder is classified as a behavioral addiction where a person becomes dependent on the overconsumption of food as a way of coping with difficult emotions, trauma, or real-life events. In response to these painful experiences, food functions as a coping mechanism, allowing the person to feel momentarily detached from themselves, their emotions, and their stress.
Numerous additional factors contribute to the onset of Binge Eating Disorder. In particular, mental health conditions such as depression, obsessive-compulsive disorder, or anxiety can be triggers, as can stress, genetic predispositions, and learned behaviors from childhood if a parent or sibling suffered from food addictions. Binge Eating Disorder is a complex addiction that often co-occurs alongside other substance abuse issues or mental health conditions.
Certain foods can also be extremely addictive, perpetuating a cycle and introducing a substance-dependency into the mix. For instance, foods high in fat and sugar can be highly addictive because they trigger the release of ‘feel-good’ chemicals such as dopamine. Dopamine is a chemical messenger in our brain that motivates us to repeat an action—in this case, eating—by producing feelings of pleasure or euphoria. When eating, the pleasure caused by the release of dopamine overrides the body’s signals of fullness or physical satisfaction, causing the person to continue eating even though they are no longer hungry.
Through our combination of medical, psychological, and personalized care, we are proud to be the only clinic in the world that addresses co-occurring conditions and every possible root cause of Binge Eating Disorder.
BingeEatingDisorder.signsTitle: What are the signs of binge eating disorder?
BingeEatingDisorder.signsDescription: Binge eating disorder is one of the most common eating disorders; however, stigma and a lack of education prevent many individuals from seeking treatment.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptoms1Title: The symptoms of binge eating disorder include:
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom1Item1: Eating in response to emotional distress, such as sadness, anxiety, or boredom.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom1Item2: Feeling compelled to eat large quantities of food, often beyond the point of physical comfort.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom1Item3: Eating more rapidly than normal during binge episodes.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom1Item4: Consuming food in secret to avoid shame or judgement.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom1Item5: Experiencing overwhelming guilt, shame, or disgust after bingeing.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom1Item6: Feeling a loss of control over eating during binge episodes.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom1Item7: Eating even when not physically hungry or when already full.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom1Item8: Frequently thinking about food or planning the next binge.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom1Item9: Stockpiling or hoarding food in anticipation of bingeing.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom1Item10: Experiencing a sense of detachment or “numbness” while bingeing.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptoms2Title: What are the psychological symptoms of binge eating?
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom2Item1: Experiencing intense cravings that feel impossible to resist.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom2Item2: Feeling a sense of emotional relief or distraction during binge episodes.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom2Item3: Increasing feelings of self-loathing or negative body image after bingeing.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom2Item4: Obsessing over binge episodes and their impact on weight or health.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom2Item5: Using food as a coping mechanism for stress or emotional pain.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptoms3Title: What are the physical symptoms of binge eating?
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom3Item1: Experiencing stomach pain or physical discomfort after consuming excessive food.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom3Item2: Rapid weight changes due to periods of bingeing.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom3Item3: Feeling lethargic or fatigued after binge episodes.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom3Item4: Developing health issues, such as high blood pressure or digestive problems, related to overeating.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptoms4Title: What are the emotional symptoms of binge eating?
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom4Item1: Feeling emotionally overwhelmed during a binge and unable to stop.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom4Item2: Experiencing mood swings or irritability linked to binge episodes.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom4Item3: Feeling trapped in a cycle of bingeing as a response to emotional distress.
BingeEatingDisorder.symptom4Item4: Developing an emotional dependency on food for comfort or escapism.
BingeEatingDisorder.howWeTreatHeading: How we treat Binge Eating Disorder
BingeEatingDisorder.howWeTreatDescription:
At Gabriel Arcángel, we understand that binge eating disorder is not simply about food; it is a complex emotional struggle that affects your physical and psychological well-being. This is why we do not follow a pre-defined treatment path. Each patient receives a bespoke treatment plan, designed to meet their specific needs and unique challenges. Your four-week stay will be thoughtfully structured to include a variety of therapeutic elements tailored to support your recovery.
Our approach focuses on both the emotional triggers that drive binge eating and the physical aspects of health, helping you build a healthier relationship with food and with yourself. Our treatments are designed to support every aspect of your recovery, ensuring you gain the tools to manage emotional triggers and develop sustainable coping strategies for long-term healing. See below for more details.
BingeEatingDisorder.section1Description:
At Gabriel Arcángel, we do not adopt a ‘one-size-fits-all’ approach. Instead, we adapt each program to every client’s unique set of needs. While our approach prioritizes cutting-edge modalities and holistic care, those individual needs ultimately guide our collaborative work.
Through a unique combination of biochemical restoration, intensive psychotherapy, and medical treatments, we are able to address the root causes of binge eating disorder on a physical, emotional, and neurobiological level. For instance, binge eating disorder may stem from physical imbalances such as hormonal dysregulation, nutrient deficiencies, or the impact of chronic stress on the body, as well as emotional factors like unresolved trauma, anxiety, or difficulties in emotional regulation. To design your specific treatment plan, we conduct extensive assessments that uncover these underlying causes, allowing us to address them effectively and comprehensively.
BingeEatingDisorder.section2Description: A team of professionals will be dedicated to you and by your side throughout your stay with us. This includes a personal psychiatrist, a medical doctor, a client relations team, and many other specialists. At the heart of this team is a live-in therapist, who is there to provide emotional support and help you address the root causes of your binge eating disorder 24/7.
BingeEatingDisorder.section3Description:
Your program will be designed under our core principles of empathy and pragmatism to create a bespoke experience that meets you exactly where you are and provides you with the treatment you need.
We provide treatment for binge eating disorder and any co-occurring mental health conditions in Querétaro.

BodyDysmorphicDisorder.conditionHeading: Body Dysmorphic Disorder: Diagnosis, Warning Signs, and Therapeutic Approach
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.conditionDescription:
At Gabriel Arcángel, we recognize how challenging Body Dysmorphic Disorder can be, and we are here to support you every step of the way. Our comprehensive treatment is designed to help you build a kinder and more compassionate relationship with your body. Our team will work closely with you to uncover any underlying issues and provide you with the tools and strategies you need to cope in a way that is right for you.
You are not alone in this; we are here to guide and support you through your journey towards healing and self-acceptance.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.whatIsDescription:
Body Dysmorphic Disorder (BDD) is a mental health condition where an individual becomes excessively preoccupied with perceived flaws in their appearance. These flaws are often minor or invisible to others, but they can cause significant distress, anxiety, and compulsive behaviors in those who suffer from them. This obsession can generate profound emotional distress and interfere with daily life.
The perceived ‘flaw’ or ‘flaws’ vary from person to person and can include any bodily feature. However, common fixations include:
Skin imperfections: Moles, pores, scars, complexion.
Face: Teeth, lips, eyes, nose, acne, wrinkles, asymmetry.
Hair: Thinning, balding, texture.
Body shape and weight: Muscles, abdomen, hips, thighs.
Another way BDD can manifest is Muscle Dysmorphia, where a person obsesses over not being muscular enough, even if they are in excellent physical shape. Bigorexia is another variation of BDD, where an individual perceives themselves as weak and thin, regardless of how muscular they become. These variations are more common in men than in women.
All forms of BDD share an intense focus on appearance and body image: constantly worrying about perceived imperfections, repeatedly checking oneself in the mirror, skin picking, or excessive grooming. For many people with BDD, shame and self-consciousness lead to the avoidance of social situations. Emotional distress in the form of anxiety, depression, and occasionally suicidal ideation is also common. It can take over your life and impact your daily functioning.
It is common for those suffering from BDD to resort to cosmetic procedures in an attempt to ‘fix’ the flaw. Despite the belief that this will solve their problems, surgeries are not a magic solution; any relief will be short-lived. Generally, symptoms return and the fixation shifts to another part of the appearance, triggering an endless cycle of seeking surgeries. In a short time, cosmetic procedures become an addiction that hinders recovery from BDD.
There is no single root cause of BDD. It is the result of a complex interplay of genetic, neurobiological, psychological, and social factors. A family history of OCD or anxiety, low self-esteem, trauma, perfectionism, beauty standards, bullying, and the use of social media filters all play a fundamental role in its development.
BDD not only affects the quality of life but can also lead to other conditions such as Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD), anxiety disorders, depression, anorexia nervosa, orthorexia, and bulimia. Additionally, to cope with stress, many turn to substance use, increasing the risk of developing an addiction.
Through our combination of medical, psychological, and personalized care, we are proud to be the clinic that addresses co-occurring conditions and every possible root cause of Body Dysmorphic Disorder.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.signsTitle: What are the signs of body dysmorphic disorder?
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.signsDescription: BDD manifests through an obsessive preoccupation with imperceptible physical flaws, driving compulsive behaviors such as constant mirror-checking or social isolation. These signs are not vanity, but indicators of a neurobiological dysregulation that generates profound emotional distress and alters the patient's daily functioning.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptoms1Title: The symptoms of binge body dysmorphic disorder:
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom1Item1: Feeling physical discomfort and mental distress when you feel “ugly” or “unattractive”.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom1Item2: Fluctuating perception about your appearance throughout the day.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom1Item3: Worrying excessively about specific parts of your body, particularly your face.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom1Item4: Compulsive mirror-checking or complete avoidance of mirrors.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom1Item5: Spending a lot of time comparing how you look to others.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom1Item6: Spending a lot of time and effort “fixing” our appearance through grooming, applying make-up or choosing clothes.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom1Item7: Going to great lengths to attempt to change your perceived flaws, for example, getting cosmetic procedures.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom1Item8: Spending excessive time/money on beauty products, dermatology treatments, or plastic surgery.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom1Item9: Believing that everything will be better once you fix that one flaw.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptoms2Title: What are the physical symptoms of BDD?
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom2Item1: Extreme preoccupation with a specific body part (e.g., nose, skin, hair, weight, muscles).
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom2Item2: Persistent negative thoughts about appearance (e.g., “I’m ugly” or “I look deformed”).
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom2Item3: Fear of judgement or humiliation over perceived flaws.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom2Item4: Emotional distress, including anxiety, depression, or suicidal thoughts.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom2Item5: Feeling “unreal” or disconnected from yourself (depersonalisation) due to excessive focus on appearance.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptoms3Title: What are the behavioural symptoms of BDD?
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom3Item1: Constantly checking mirrors or avoiding them altogether.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom3Item2: Excessive grooming, skin-picking, or makeup use to “fix” perceived flaws.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom3Item3: Seeking cosmetic procedures or surgeries but feeling dissatisfied afterward.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom3Item4: Experiencing muscle dysmorphia, an obsession with body size/muscle mass.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom3Item5: Frequent comparison of one’s appearance to others.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom4Item1: Constant reassurance-seeking from others about looks.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom4Item2: Excessive exercise, dieting, or use of supplements (especially in muscle dysmorphia).
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom4Item3: Social withdrawal (avoiding events, work, or school due to appearance concerns).
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom4Item4: Changing clothes multiple times to cover up perceived flaws.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom4Item5: Avoiding photos or filtering/editing images excessively.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.symptom4Item6: Picking at your skin to make it “smooth”.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.howWeTreatHeading: How we treat Body Dysmorphic Disorder
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.howWeTreatDescription:
At Gabriel Arcángel, we understand that Body Dysmorphic Disorder is deeply personal and unique to each individual. This is why we do not follow a pre-defined treatment path. Instead, every person who comes to us receives a bespoke treatment plan, designed specifically to address their unique challenges. Each day is meticulously structured, incorporating a range of therapeutic elements carefully selected to support their healing process.
Our therapies work in unison to ensure that all aspects of your physical, emotional, and psychological well-being are addressed, helping you build a healthier relationship with your body and mind. See below for more details.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.section1Description:
At Gabriel Arcángel, we adopt a multidisciplinary, 360-degree approach to treating Body Dysmorphic Disorder, combining specialized psychotherapy, cutting-edge medical treatments, and complementary therapies to support your journey towards healing. Our approach begins with an in-depth assessment procedure to uncover the underlying physical, emotional, and psychological factors contributing to your condition.
Based on these findings, we develop a highly personalized treatment plan, which may include therapies such as Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), trauma-focused care, and mindfulness techniques, alongside biochemical restoration and medical interventions designed to restore balance to your body and mind.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.section2Description:
We are the only eating disorder treatment center in Querétaro to assign a team of dedicated professionals to each client.
By your side throughout your treatment program is a solid team of doctors, therapists, and eating disorder specialists. Your team will provide unparalleled, around-the-clock support to guide you on your road to recovery. Additionally, a live-in therapist will be available for 24/7 emotional support.
BodyDysmorphicDisorder.section3Description:
At Gabriel Arcángel, we tailor-make our comprehensive and intensive Body Dysmorphic Disorder treatment programs to suit your specific needs. Based on our core principles of empathy and pragmatism, we design the treatment to meet you exactly where you are.
We provide treatment for Body Dysmorphic Disorder and any co-occurring mental health conditions at our private eating disorder treatment clinic in Querétaro.
